meta.purpose: Misunderstandings, banned terms, and self-checks for short-video subtitle judgment.

misunderstandings.wrong: If a subtitle line is ambiguous, always rewrite it for clarity.
misunderstandings.correct: Ambiguity is sometimes the speaker's intended effect. Rewriting for clarity can erase personality. Only clarify when the ambiguity causes genuine misunderstanding, not when it's a stylistic choice.
misunderstandings.why: Over-clarifying strips the speaker's personality, making branded content feel anonymous.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Stylistic ambiguity (speaker's voice) vs. harmful ambiguity (viewer will misunderstand the content).
misunderstandings.failure_risk: Sanitizing every ambiguous phrase produces subtitles that read like corporate copy.

misunderstandings.wrong: Shorter subtitle lines are always better because they're easier to read.
misunderstandings.correct: A line that is too short but breaks the speaker's emotional rhythm is worse than a slightly longer line that preserves the flow. The viewer's emotional connection matters more than optimal character count.
misunderstandings.why: Optimizing for character count produces emotionally disconnected subtitles.

misunderstandings.wrong: The same subtitle style works for all audiences — just keep it clean and readable.
misunderstandings.correct: Different audiences have different reading speeds, attention patterns, and expectations. A subtitle style tuned for Douyin teenagers will alienate WeChat Channels seniors, and vice versa.
misunderstandings.why: One-size-fits-all subtitles serve no specific audience well and feel off-brand.

banned_terms.why: Vague direction that doesn't help the agent decide what to change.
Too generic — readability means different things for different audiences.

banned_terms.replace_with: align with account voice and audience expectations
banned_terms.why: Implies surface-level edit when subtitles need judgment-level decisions.

banned_terms.replace_with: split at the speaker's natural pauses
banned_terms.why: Mechanical character-count splitting ignores emotional rhythm.

banned_terms.term: you don't want to miss this
banned_terms.why: Manufactures urgency and erodes trust when overused.
Anxiety-driving language that positions the audience as deficient.

self_check: Does this subtitle break respect the speaker's emotional beat?
Would this tone feel right for our target audience?
Did I preserve the speaker's voice, or did I sanitize it?
Is any line using fear or urgency language unnecessarily?
Would a viewer feel the pacing is too fast or too slow?
